patterns_found.pattern_name: Normalized User Profile with Identities Array
patterns_found.description: Auth0 stores a single user profile with an array of linked identities, each containing provider, connection, user_id, and isSocial fields.
patterns_found.implementation_details: User profile has core fields (user_id, name, nickname, email, email_verified, picture) plus an identities[] array. Each identity element has: connection (string), provider (string), user_id (string unique per connection), isSocial (boolean). In the most common case the array has one element; linked accounts add more elements. Additional provider-specific attributes pass through unchanged.

data_model.relationships: User 1:N Identity (each identity = one provider link). Connection defines the provider type and configuration.

patterns_found.business_value: Provides a proven pattern for modeling multi-provider identity with a single user record and linked provider identities.

patterns_found.description: Auth0 recommends Universal Login as the single entry point for all authentication flows, using Authorization Code + PKCE for all client types.
patterns_found.implementation_details: Universal Login provides SSO out of the box. PKCE is mandatory for public clients (SPAs, mobile). OAuth 2.1 makes PKCE mandatory for all flows. The flow: generate code_verifier (43-128 chars CSPRNG) -> SHA256 hash as code_challenge -> send to /authorize -> exchange code + code_verifier at /oauth/token. This prevents authorization code interception attacks.

data_model.relationships: AuthorizationCode is bound to CodeChallenge at issuance, verified with CodeVerifier at exchange.

patterns_found.business_value: Industry standard for securing OAuth flows against code interception. Required for OAuth 2.1 compliance.

patterns_found.pattern_name: Refresh Token Rotation with Token Families
patterns_found.description: Auth0 implements refresh token rotation where each use issues a new refresh token, and reuse detection invalidates the entire token family.
patterns_found.implementation_details: Each refresh token is single-use. On exchange, a new access + refresh token pair is issued. Token families group all refresh tokens from an initial authentication. If a previously-rotated token is presented (reuse), the entire family is invalidated, forcing re-authentication. Access token lifetime: 15-30 min. Refresh token lifetime: 7-30 days. The jti claim provides unique token identifiers for tracking.

data_model.relationships: TokenFamily 1:N RefreshToken chain. Each RefreshToken links to its predecessor (parent_token_id).

patterns_found.business_value: Detects token theft by identifying reuse of already-rotated tokens. Critical for security in mobile and SPA scenarios.

patterns_found.description: Auth0 implements step-up auth where certain high-risk operations require additional authentication factors beyond the initial login.
patterns_found.implementation_details: MFA is triggered based on: resource sensitivity (e.g., financial transactions), risk signals (new device, unusual location, time-of-day anomaly), or explicit policy (admin actions). The system uses acr (Authentication Context Class Reference) and amr (Authentication Methods Reference) claims in tokens to indicate the authentication strength. Step-up re-prompts for MFA or password confirmation without full re-authentication. Risk-based auth can be rule-based, ML-based, or hybrid.

data_model.relationships: Policy defines which risk signals trigger step-up. MFAFactor is enrolled per user.

patterns_found.business_value: Balances security and UX by only requiring additional factors for high-risk operations.

patterns_found.description: Keycloak uses Realms as isolated identity namespaces with built-in identity brokering to delegate authentication to external IdPs.
patterns_found.implementation_details: A Realm contains: users, credentials, roles, groups, identity providers, clients, and authentication flows. Identity Brokering works as hub-and-spoke: all login paths lead to Keycloak as the central authority. External IdPs (OIDC, SAML, Social) are configured per-realm. Token/claim mappers transform external schemas to internal representation. Users can have federated identities from multiple providers. Authentication flows are composable and support conditional step-up. Keycloak supports LDAP/AD federation with on-demand sync or periodic batch sync.

data_model.relationships: Realm 1:N Users. User 1:N Credentials. User 1:N FederatedIdentities. Realm 1:N IdentityProviders. Realm 1:N Clients. User N:M Roles. User N:M Groups. Group N:M Roles (role inheritance).

patterns_found.business_value: The most comprehensive open-source IAM data model. Demonstrates how to separate realms, identity providers, federated identities, and credentials as distinct entities.

patterns_found.pattern_name: Configurable Token Lifecycle with Offline Access
patterns_found.description: Keycloak provides fine-grained token lifetime configuration with separate SSO session, client session, and offline token settings.
patterns_found.implementation_details: Token lifetimes configured at realm level: Access Token Lifespan (default 5 min, recommended 5-15 min). SSO Session Idle (30 min sliding). SSO Session Max (10 hours absolute). Refresh Token Max Reuse set to 0 for rotation. Offline tokens allow refresh without active session (for mobile). Three token types: Identity Token (user info claims), Access Token (authorization), Refresh Token (session continuity). Token mappers customize claims per client. Back-channel logout notifies all clients when session ends.

data_model.relationships: SSOSession 1:N ClientSessions. SSOSession has idle and absolute timeout. ClientSession bound to specific client within SSO session.

patterns_found.business_value: Demonstrates production-grade token lifecycle with both sliding and absolute timeouts, offline access for mobile, and fine-grained per-client configuration.

patterns_found.description: Keycloak models authentication as composable flows where steps can be conditional, required, optional, or alternative.
patterns_found.implementation_details: Authentication flows define credential requirements for different scenarios: browser login, registration, credential reset, identity provider first-login. Flows support conditional execution (e.g., if user has OTP configured, require it). Step-up auth triggers additional factors based on context. Each flow step is an Authenticator SPI implementation. Flows can branch: alternative (any one succeeds), required (all must succeed), conditional (evaluated at runtime). This enables: password-only for low risk, password+OTP for medium, password+WebAuthn for high.

data_model.relationships: Flow 1:N Executions (ordered). Execution references an Authenticator. Flow can contain sub-flows.

patterns_found.business_value: Demonstrates how to model flexible authentication policies that can adapt to different risk levels and provider types.

patterns_found.description: Supabase automatically links identities with the same verified email address to a single user, preventing duplicate accounts across providers.
patterns_found.implementation_details: When a new OAuth user signs in, Supabase looks for existing users with the same email. If found, the new identity is linked automatically. Security controls: (1) Only verified emails can auto-link (prevents pre-account takeover). (2) Unconfirmed identities are removed when a confirmed identity links. (3) SAML SSO users are excluded from auto-linking. (4) Duplicate email signup attempts return obfuscated responses to prevent enumeration. Manual linking via linkIdentity() for cross-email linking. unlinkIdentity() requires at least 2 linked identities. Native OAuth linking via linkIdentityWithIdToken() for mobile (Google, Apple, Facebook, Kakao, Keycloak).

data_model.relationships: User 1:N Identities. Each Identity has a provider + provider_id + email. User 1:N Sessions. User 1:N Factors.

patterns_found.business_value: Clean automatic linking pattern that handles the common case (same email, different providers) while preventing account takeover attacks via unverified email matching.

patterns_found.pattern_name: Explicit Account Linking with Conflict Resolution
patterns_found.description: Firebase requires explicit user-initiated account linking with built-in conflict resolution when credentials are already in use by another account.
patterns_found.implementation_details: Flow: (1) Sign in user via any provider. (2) Get credential from new provider (up to but not including signIn). (3) Call linkWithCredential(credential) to link. On conflict (credential already used by another account): fetchSignInMethodsForEmail() to find existing providers -> prompt user to sign in with existing provider -> then link the new credential. Unlinking a provider and re-signing creates a NEW separate account (not re-link). Each user has a providerData[] array listing linked providers. Supports: Google, Facebook, Twitter, GitHub, phone, email/password. The user.uid remains stable across all provider links.

data_model.relationships: User 1:N ProviderData entries. Each provider entry has: providerId, uid (provider-specific), displayName, email, photoURL, phoneNumber.

patterns_found.business_value: Demonstrates explicit linking with robust conflict handling. The pattern of stable user.uid across all providers is critical for maintaining referential integrity in downstream systems.

patterns_found.description: Clerk uses a dual-token model: a long-lived client token (stateful, HttpOnly cookie) and an ultra-short-lived session token (60s JWT) to balance security and performance.
patterns_found.implementation_details: Client Token (__client cookie): long-lived, HttpOnly, stored on FAPI domain, contains client identifier + anti-fixation rotating token, serves as source of truth. Session Token (__session cookie): 60-second JWT, non-HttpOnly (accessible to client JS for API calls), auto-refreshed every 50 seconds via background fetch to /client/sessions/{id}/tokens. This decouples token expiration from session lifetime. The 60s window means: even if JWT is stolen, it is usable for max 60 seconds. No need for blacklists/revocation lists for access tokens. Server verification can be fully stateless (JWT validation only).

data_model.relationships: Client 1:N Sessions. User 1:N ExternalAccounts (linked providers). Session belongs to User via Client.

patterns_found.business_value: Ultra-short JWT lifetime (60s) eliminates the need for token revocation infrastructure while maintaining stateless verification. The background refresh pattern ensures seamless UX.

patterns_found.description: Clerk natively supports multiple simultaneous authenticated sessions from the same browser, allowing users to switch between accounts without re-authentication.
patterns_found.implementation_details: Multiple accounts can be signed in simultaneously in the same browser. Users can switch between accounts seamlessly. The Client token tracks all active sessions. Each session has its own session token (60s JWT). Session lifecycle management includes active device monitoring and session revocation. Configurable session options: session lifetime, max concurrent sessions, and token customization per application.

data_model.relationships: Client 1:N Sessions (multiple concurrent). Each Session independent with own JWT.

patterns_found.business_value: Multi-session is increasingly expected in B2B SaaS. The pattern of session-per-account-per-device provides clear session isolation.

patterns_found.description: FusionAuth provides six distinct linking strategies that determine how external IdP identities map to internal user accounts.
patterns_found.implementation_details: Strategies: (1) Anonymous Link - creates minimal user without email/username. (2) Link On Email (Create) - auto-creates user matching provider email. (3) Link On Email (Require Existing) - links only if email already exists. (4) Link On Username (Create) - auto-creates by username. (5) Link On Username (Require Existing) - links only to existing username. (6) Pending Link - requires manual user confirmation. (7) Disabled - defers to API for programmatic control. Each provider configured independently. Registration is separate from user creation (user can exist without app registration). Lambda-based reconciliation for custom attribute mapping from provider tokens. Tenant-scoped provider configuration.

data_model.relationships: User 1:N IdentityProviderLinks. User 1:N UserRegistrations (per-app). IdentityProvider belongs to Tenant. Link has provider_user_id + provider_id.

patterns_found.business_value: Most flexible linking strategy model in the market. The separation of user creation from application registration is valuable for multi-application scenarios. Pending Link strategy handles the edge case where email-based auto-linking is insufficient.

patterns_found.pattern_name: Zanzibar-Based Fine-Grained Authorization with Session Integration
patterns_found.description: WorkOS implements Google Zanzibar-style FGA that integrates with authentication, embedding organization-level roles and permissions directly in access tokens.
patterns_found.implementation_details: FGA extends RBAC with relationship-based access control (ReBAC). Permissions flow down resource hierarchy automatically (workspace admin -> project access -> app access). Organization roles embedded in JWT access tokens for instant checks without API calls. Resource-scoped roles managed via API separately from org roles. IdP group-to-role mapping: Okta/Azure AD groups auto-map to organization roles. Schema language defines authorization model (types, relations, rules). Warrants (tuples) define actual access grants: (subject, relation, object).

data_model.relationships: Subject -[Relation]-> Resource. Permissions inherit through resource hierarchy. Warrants are the actual permission tuples.

patterns_found.business_value: Shows how authorization can be deeply integrated with authentication tokens. Embedding roles in JWTs eliminates per-request authorization lookups for common cases.

patterns_found.description: OWASP defines comprehensive requirements for credential storage, session management, error handling, and multi-factor authentication.
patterns_found.implementation_details: Password hashing: Argon2id (preferred), min config m=19MiB, t=2, p=1. Higher security: m=46MiB, t=1, p=1. Fallback: scrypt (2^17 cost, block 8, p=1), bcrypt (work factor 10+, 72-byte limit), PBKDF2 (600K iterations HMAC-SHA256 for FIPS). Password length: min 8 with MFA, min 15 without MFA, max 64+. No composition rules. Use breach databases (Pwned Passwords). No forced rotation unless breach. Error messages: generic always ('Login failed; Invalid user ID or password'). Prevent user enumeration via consistent response timing. Account lockout: threshold + observation window + lockout duration. Consider exponential lockout (1s, 2s, 4s...). MFA: 'would have stopped 99.9% of account compromises'. Implement wherever feasible. Reauthentication for sensitive operations. Risk-based auth: IP, geolocation, fingerprint, time-of-day scoring.
patterns_found.business_value: Defines the baseline security requirements that any IAM implementation must meet to pass a security audit.

patterns_found.description: OWASP defines requirements for session ID generation, binding, timeouts, and cookie configuration.
patterns_found.implementation_details: Session ID: min 64 bits entropy from CSPRNG, at least 16 hex chars, meaningless content, generic names. Cookie attributes: Secure (HTTPS only), HttpOnly (no JS access), SameSite (CSRF prevention), narrow Path/Domain scope, non-persistent (no Max-Age for sessions). Timeouts: Idle 2-5 min (high value) or 15-30 min (low risk). Absolute 4-8 hours. Renewal timeout: periodically regenerate session ID mid-session. Session binding: tie to client IP, User-Agent, digital certificates. Session fixation: regenerate ID after every privilege level change. Concurrent sessions: decide policy (terminate previous, prompt user, allow multiple). Logging: track full session lifecycle.
patterns_found.business_value: Critical for security audit compliance. The idle/absolute timeout model and session binding requirements directly inform session entity design.

patterns_found.description: NIST defines three assurance levels with specific authenticator and session requirements. The 2025 revision (SP 800-63B-4) integrates passkeys and mandates phishing-resistant MFA at AAL2+.
patterns_found.implementation_details: AAL1: Single-factor authentication. Password or possession-based. Session max 12 hours (moderate) or 30 days with reauthentication. AAL2: Multi-factor required. Phishing-resistant MFA strongly promoted. FIDO2 passkeys (both device-bound and syncable) now explicitly integrated. Session max 12 hours. AAL3: Hardware-bound authenticator required (e.g., hardware security key). Verifier impersonation resistance mandatory. Session max 1 hour. Syncable passkeys supplement: NIST SP 800-63Bsup1 provides guidance on syncable authenticators. Session binding requirements increase with AAL. Token binding to client characteristics recommended at AAL2+. PCI DSS: 15 min inactivity timeout. HIPAA: automatic logoff. SOX: session audit trails. GDPR: immediate session termination on data deletion.
patterns_found.business_value: Defines compliance-driven requirements for session lifetimes and authenticator types. The AAL framework provides a clear model for step-up authentication policy.

patterns_found.description: Telegram Mini Apps use a signed initData payload for user authentication, validated via a two-step HMAC-SHA256 chain.
patterns_found.implementation_details: InitData structure: auth_date (unix timestamp), hash (signature), user (JSON object with id, first_name, last_name, username, language_code, is_premium, allows_write_to_pm, photo_url), query_id, chat_instance, chat_type, start_param, can_send_after. Validation algorithm: (1) Extract all key=value pairs except hash. (2) Sort alphabetically. (3) intermediate_key = HMAC-SHA256('WebAppData', bot_token). (4) computed_hash = HMAC-SHA256(sorted_pairs_joined_by_newline, intermediate_key). (5) Compare computed_hash with provided hash. Freshness: Check auth_date against server time, reject if >1 hour old to prevent replay. Transmission: Send as Authorization header 'tma {initDataRaw}'. Server-side validation only. NEVER trust client-side validation.

data_model.relationships: InitData contains one WebAppUser. WebAppUser identified by telegram_id (bigint).

patterns_found.business_value: Directly relevant to the existing system's Telegram auth implementation. The freshness check (1 hour) and HMAC chain are security-critical.

sources.name: JWT Security Best Practices (Phase Two / OWASP)

patterns_found.pattern_name: JWT Signing Algorithm Selection and Validation
patterns_found.description: Comprehensive guidance on choosing between HMAC (HS256), RSA (RS256), and ECC (ES256/EdDSA) algorithms, plus critical validation requirements.
patterns_found.implementation_details: Algorithm selection: HMAC (HS256/384/512) - fastest, but shared secret means any holder can generate tokens. Single-service only. RSA (RS256+) - asymmetric, distributed validation, min 2048-bit keys, slower. ECC (ES256+) - recommended for new implementations, smaller keys, faster than RSA, 2.5x slower signing than HMAC. EdDSA (Ed25519) - newest, very fast, good security. For distributed systems (microservices): use asymmetric (RS256 or ES256). For single-service monoliths: HS256 acceptable. Critical validation: (1) Reject alg:none. (2) Enforce allowlisted algorithms only. (3) Validate exp, iat, nbf, aud, iss claims. (4) Check jti for revocation. (5) Verify signature against public key/JWKS. (6) Allow ~60s clock skew. Token storage: Web = HttpOnly + Secure + SameSite=Strict cookies. Mobile = OS keychain/keystore. NEVER localStorage. Revocation: short lifetimes (15-30 min) + refresh tokens + distributed blacklists for emergency. Key rotation: monthly/quarterly with kid header for transition. JWKS endpoint for multiple concurrent keys.

data_model.relationships: Header declares alg + kid. Payload contains claims. Signature binds header+payload with key.

patterns_found.business_value: Directly applicable to the current HS256 implementation. Provides upgrade path to asymmetric algorithms if the system evolves to microservices.

patterns_found.pattern_name: Comprehensive Token Lifecycle with Revocation Strategies
patterns_found.description: Four token revocation methods compared: blocklists, short-lived tokens, back-channel logout, and token versioning.
patterns_found.implementation_details: Access token lifetime: 15-60 min. Refresh token: 30-90 days. Revocation methods: (1) Token Blocklists - centralized revoked token list, strong security, medium complexity, potential latency via Redis/similar. (2) Short-Lived Tokens - 15-30 min access tokens reduce damage window, not true revocation. (3) Back-Channel Logout - federated systems notify all clients when session ends, high complexity. (4) Token Versioning - tokens carry version ID, increment on security events (password reset, role change) to invalidate all prior tokens instantly. For single-service monolith: token versioning is simplest. For federated: back-channel logout. For general use: blocklist in Redis with TTL matching token expiration. Critical: never log actual token values, only metadata. All microservices must enforce identical validation logic.

data_model.relationships: TokenBlocklist stores jti values with TTL. TokenVersion per-user increments on security events.

patterns_found.business_value: Token versioning pattern is elegant for monoliths: store a version counter on the Identity, increment on security events, include in JWT, reject tokens with old versions.

patterns_found.description: WebAuthn combines W3C browser API (WebAuthn) with CTAP2 authenticator protocol, supporting both platform authenticators (Face ID, Windows Hello) and roaming authenticators (YubiKey).
patterns_found.implementation_details: Registration flow: (1) Server generates challenge (random bytes). (2) Browser calls navigator.credentials.create() with challenge + relying party info. (3) Authenticator creates key pair, returns public key + attestation. (4) Server stores public key as credential. Authentication flow: (1) Server generates challenge. (2) Browser calls navigator.credentials.get(). (3) Authenticator signs challenge with private key. (4) Server verifies signature with stored public key. Credential types: (a) Platform authenticators (built-in biometrics). (b) Syncable passkeys (stored in cloud keychain - Apple, Google, Microsoft). (c) Hardware security keys (YubiKey, Titan). Storage: credential_id (unique), public_key, sign_count (replay detection), transports, user_verified flag. NIST SP 800-63B-4 now integrates passkeys at AAL2 (syncable) and AAL3 (device-bound). Can serve as both first factor (passwordless) and second factor (with password).

data_model.relationships: User 1:N WebAuthnCredentials. Each credential has unique credential_id + public_key.

patterns_found.business_value: Passkeys are the future of authentication per NIST 2025 guidance. Planning for WebAuthn credential storage in the data model future-proofs the system.

patterns_found.pattern_name: Identity as Aggregate Root with Credential Value Objects
patterns_found.description: In DDD, Identity is modeled as the aggregate root of the IAM bounded context, with credentials, sessions, and provider links as owned entities or value objects.
patterns_found.implementation_details: Identity aggregate root: id (UUID), type (LOCAL|OIDC|TELEGRAM|PASSKEY), account_type (CUSTOMER|STAFF), is_active, created_at, updated_at. Owned entities within the aggregate: LocalCredentials (email + password_hash), TelegramCredentials (telegram_id + profile), LinkedAccount (provider + provider_sub_id), Session (refresh_token_hash + device info + expiry). Value objects: Email (validated format), PasswordHash (encapsulates hashing), TokenHash, DeviceFingerprint. Domain events: IdentityRegistered, IdentityAuthenticated, ProviderLinked, ProviderUnlinked, SessionCreated, SessionRevoked, PasswordChanged, MFAEnrolled, IdentityDeactivated. Cross-context communication via events only. User module consumes IdentityRegistered to create Customer/Staff. The Identity aggregate boundary ensures all credential changes, session operations, and provider links are transactionally consistent.

data_model.relationships: Identity 1:1 LocalCredentials (optional). Identity 1:1 TelegramCredentials (optional). Identity 1:N LinkedAccounts. Identity 1:N Sessions. Identity N:M Roles.

patterns_found.business_value: Directly maps to the existing codebase structure. The Identity aggregate with owned credential entities is exactly the pattern already partially implemented.

patterns_found.pattern_name: CQRS for Authentication Commands and Queries
patterns_found.description: Authentication operations split into commands (Login, Register, LinkProvider, RevokeSession) and queries (GetSession, ListSessions, GetPermissions).
patterns_found.implementation_details: Commands: LoginCommand (email/password or provider token -> session + tokens). RegisterCommand (create identity + credentials). LinkProviderCommand (add provider to existing identity). RefreshTokenCommand (rotate tokens). RevokeSessionCommand. ChangePasswordCommand. EnrollMFACommand. Queries: GetSessionQuery (resolve session from token). ListActiveSessionsQuery (for session management UI). GetPermissionsQuery (cache-aside with Redis). GetIdentityProfileQuery. Command handlers own the write model (domain entities). Query handlers can use optimized read models (denormalized views). Events flow to other bounded contexts for eventual consistency. No direct imports between modules.

data_model.relationships: Each Command maps to one CommandHandler. Each Query maps to one QueryHandler. CommandHandlers operate on Aggregates. QueryHandlers use ReadModels.

patterns_found.business_value: Validates the existing CQRS pattern in the codebase and provides guidance on which additional commands/queries should exist for a complete IAM system.

patterns_found.description: RFC 9700 (2025) consolidates OAuth 2.0 security best practices including mandatory PKCE, sender-constrained tokens, and redirect URI restrictions.
patterns_found.implementation_details: Key requirements: (1) PKCE mandatory for all authorization code flows (not just public clients). (2) PKCE downgrade attacks possible when optional - servers must enforce. (3) Exact redirect URI matching (no wildcards). (4) State parameter for CSRF in GET redirects. (5) HTTPS mandatory everywhere. (6) Access token lifetimes: 15 min - 1 hour. (7) Refresh tokens: finite expiration, rotation recommended. (8) Sender-constrained tokens (DPoP or mTLS) for high-security. (9) Authorization code: one-time use, short-lived (10 min max). (10) Client authentication: confidential clients must authenticate at token endpoint.
patterns_found.business_value: The latest RFC (2025) that any OAuth implementation must comply with. Mandatory PKCE and exact redirect URI matching are non-negotiable for security audits.

patterns_found.pattern_name: Session Store with Device Metadata and Sliding Expiry
patterns_found.description: Production session management stores sessions server-side with device metadata, sliding idle timeout, absolute max timeout, and supports concurrent session policies.
patterns_found.implementation_details: Session record fields: session_id, identity_id, device_fingerprint (hash of User-Agent + screen + timezone + plugins), ip_address, user_agent, created_at, last_active_at, idle_expires_at (sliding), absolute_expires_at, is_revoked, activated_roles. Sliding expiry: extend idle_expires_at on each valid request. Absolute expiry: never extend regardless of activity. Device fingerprinting: hash client environment attributes, verify on each request, trigger re-auth on mismatch. Concurrent session policy options: (a) Allow unlimited. (b) Fixed maximum (e.g., 5 per user). (c) Terminate oldest on new login. (d) Prompt user to choose. Session storage: Redis for fast lookup (session_id -> session data), PostgreSQL for persistence and audit trail. Minimum session fields per OWASP: session ID, user ID, device/client metadata, issued-at, expiry, authorization snapshot.

data_model.relationships: User 1:N Sessions. Session has one DeviceFingerprint.

patterns_found.business_value: The existing Session entity has most fields but lacks sliding expiry (last_active_at, idle_expires_at) and device fingerprinting. These are critical for production-grade session security.

common_patterns.pattern: Single User Identity with Multiple Provider Links
common_patterns.consensus_approach: One user/identity record owns an array/collection of linked provider identities. Each link stores: provider name, provider-specific user ID, and optionally email. The user's primary ID (UUID) remains stable across all provider links. This is the universal pattern across all IAM systems.

common_patterns.pattern: Email-Based Automatic Account Linking with Verification Guard
common_patterns.consensus_approach: When a user authenticates via a new provider, automatically link to an existing account if the email matches AND is verified by both the existing account and the new provider. Unverified emails must never auto-link to prevent pre-account takeover attacks. Manual/explicit linking is available as a fallback for cross-email scenarios.

common_patterns.pattern: Short-Lived Access Token + Rotating Refresh Token
common_patterns.consensus_approach: Access tokens: 5-60 minutes (consensus: 15 min). Refresh tokens: 7-90 days, single-use with rotation. On each refresh, issue new access + refresh token pair and invalidate the old refresh token. Store refresh token hash (SHA-256) server-side, never the raw token. Reuse detection: if a previously-rotated token is presented, invalidate the entire token family.

common_patterns.pattern: HttpOnly Secure Cookie Storage for Web Tokens
common_patterns.consensus_approach: Web applications must store tokens in HttpOnly + Secure + SameSite=Strict cookies. Never use localStorage (XSS vulnerable). BFF (Backend-for-Frontend) pattern: proxy auth through server, set cookies from server-side, client never sees raw tokens. Mobile: use OS keychain/keystore.

Argon2id is the recommended algorithm. Minimum parameters: m=19MiB, t=2, p=1. Higher security: m=46MiB, t=1, p=1. Fallback chain: scrypt > bcrypt > PBKDF2. Bcrypt limited to 72 bytes. Use pepper for defense in depth. No forced password rotation unless breach confirmed.

Identity bounded context emits events consumed by other modules: IdentityRegistered (-> create User/Customer), IdentityAuthenticated (-> audit log), ProviderLinked/Unlinked (-> update profile), SessionCreated/Revoked (-> device management), PasswordChanged (-> notify user), IdentityDeactivated (-> GDPR cleanup). Events published via transactional outbox for guaranteed delivery.

common_patterns.pattern: Idle + Absolute Session Timeout Dual Model
common_patterns.consensus_approach: Two independent timeouts: Idle timeout (extends on activity, 15-30 min for normal apps, 2-5 min for high-value). Absolute timeout (never extends, 4-8 hours for web, 12 hours NIST moderate, 1 hour NIST high assurance). Session must be terminated when either timeout is reached. PCI DSS requires 15-min idle timeout for payment systems.

common_patterns.pattern: PKCE Mandatory for All OAuth Flows
common_patterns.consensus_approach: PKCE is now mandatory for ALL authorization code flows (not just public clients). This is codified in RFC 9700 (2025) and the upcoming OAuth 2.1. Authorization servers must enforce PKCE. Downgrade attacks are possible when PKCE is optional. Code verifier: 43-128 chars CSPRNG. Challenge method: S256 (SHA-256).

unique_insights.insight: Clerk's 60-second JWT session token eliminates the need for token revocation infrastructure entirely. The ultra-short lifetime means stolen tokens are useless within a minute, and the background 50-second refresh cycle is transparent to the user.
unique_insights.potential_value: Could simplify the current architecture by removing the need for Redis-based token blacklisting if access token lifetime is reduced to 60 seconds. Trade-off: higher /token endpoint load.

unique_insights.insight: FusionAuth's 'Pending Link' strategy handles the case where automatic email matching is unreliable by deferring the linking decision to the user. This is particularly valuable for systems where users might have different emails across providers.
unique_insights.potential_value: Relevant for the Telegram auth scenario where users may not have email at all. A pending link flow could allow Telegram users to later link their email/OIDC account.

unique_insights.insight: Token Versioning as a revocation mechanism: store a version counter on the Identity entity, increment on security events (password change, role change, force logout), include in JWT claims. Any token with a lower version is instantly rejected without hitting a blacklist.
unique_insights.potential_value: Elegant alternative to Redis-based blacklists for the monolith architecture. Can be implemented as a simple integer field on Identity, checked during JWT validation against the database-backed value.

unique_insights.insight: WorkOS embeds organization-level roles and permissions directly in JWT access tokens, enabling instant authorization checks without per-request API calls to a permission service.
unique_insights.potential_value: The existing system already caches permissions in Redis with a cache-aside pattern. Embedding a permission snapshot in the JWT could eliminate Redis lookups for common cases, with Redis as fallback for cache misses.

unique_insights.insight: NIST SP 800-63B-4 (2025) now explicitly integrates syncable passkeys at AAL2 and device-bound passkeys at AAL3, making FIDO2/WebAuthn the gold standard for phishing-resistant authentication.
unique_insights.potential_value: Planning the data model to include WebAuthn credential storage (credential_id, public_key, sign_count, transports) future-proofs the system for passkey adoption without schema changes.

anti_patterns.reason: localStorage is accessible to any JavaScript running on the page, making it vulnerable to XSS attacks. A single XSS vulnerability exposes all stored tokens. HttpOnly cookies are immune to JavaScript access.

A stolen non-rotating refresh token can be used indefinitely until expiration. Without rotation and reuse detection, there is no way to detect token theft. Always use single-use refresh tokens with family-based reuse detection.

Pre-account takeover attack: attacker registers with victim's email via a provider that does not verify email, then when the real user signs up, their account is automatically linked to the attacker's. Only link on verified emails.

anti_patterns.pattern: Detailed error messages revealing authentication state
anti_patterns.reason: Returning specific errors like 'email not found' vs 'wrong password' enables user enumeration attacks. All authentication failures must return the same generic message with consistent response timing.

InitData validation must be performed exclusively server-side. Client-side validation can be bypassed, allowing forged requests impersonating any user. The HMAC chain with bot_token must never be exposed to the client.
The 'none' algorithm allows unsigned JWTs to pass validation, effectively disabling all token security. Always enforce an allowlist of acceptable algorithms and reject any token not matching.

anti_patterns.pattern: Single aggregate boundary for Identity + User profile
anti_patterns.reason: Mixing authentication concerns (credentials, sessions) with user profile data (name, preferences) in a single aggregate creates unnecessary coupling. Identity and User should be separate bounded contexts communicating via domain events, even if they share a primary key.
Forced rotation leads to weaker passwords (users choose predictable patterns). NIST and OWASP both recommend rotation only after confirmed breach. Encourage strong passwords + MFA instead.

industry_standards.relevance: Mandatory for any OIDC provider integration. PKCE required for all flows. Exact redirect URI matching. Short-lived authorization codes. Sender-constrained tokens for high-security.
industry_standards.applies_when: Any external identity provider (Google, GitHub, etc.) is integrated via OIDC/OAuth.

industry_standards.relevance: Standard protocol for identity federation. Defines ID Token structure (JWT with sub, iss, aud, exp, iat, auth_time, nonce). Provides UserInfo endpoint for profile data. Discovery mechanism (.well-known/openid-configuration).
industry_standards.applies_when: The system acts as a relying party accepting tokens from external OIDC providers.

industry_standards.relevance: Defines AAL1/2/3 requirements. AAL2 requires phishing-resistant MFA (FIDO2 passkeys integrated). Session lifetime limits per assurance level. Passkey guidance via supplement SP 800-63Bsup1.
industry_standards.applies_when: System needs to meet government or enterprise compliance requirements for authentication assurance.

industry_standards.standard: OWASP Authentication & Session Management Cheat Sheets
industry_standards.relevance: Baseline security requirements: Argon2id hashing, generic error messages, account lockout, session binding, cookie security attributes, idle/absolute timeouts. Required for any security audit.
industry_standards.applies_when: Always. These are minimum security requirements for any production authentication system.

industry_standards.relevance: Browser API for passkey/hardware key authentication. Registration + authentication ceremony flows. Credential storage: credential_id + public_key + sign_count. Phishing-resistant by design.
industry_standards.applies_when: System needs to support passkeys or hardware security keys as authentication factors.

industry_standards.relevance: Telegram-specific authentication standard. Two-step HMAC chain using bot_token. Freshness check via auth_date (max 1 hour). Server-side validation only.
industry_standards.applies_when: System authenticates users from Telegram Mini App client.

key_questions_addressed.question: How do market leaders (Auth0, Keycloak, Supabase) model the Identity aggregate with multiple authentication providers?
key_questions_addressed.summary: All leaders use a single user/identity record with a 1:N collection of provider links. Auth0: identities[] array with {provider, connection, user_id}. Keycloak: FederatedIdentity entities per user. Supabase: Identity table with user_id FK. Firebase: providerData[] array. FusionAuth: IdentityProviderLink table. The universal pattern is: stable internal UUID + linked external provider credentials. The existing codebase's Identity + LinkedAccount + TelegramCredentials structure already follows this pattern correctly.

key_questions_addressed.question: What are best practices for JWT access token structure (claims, lifetime, signing algorithm) and refresh token rotation?
key_questions_addressed.summary: Access token: 15 min lifetime (consensus), claims: sub (user ID), sid (session ID), jti (unique ID), iss, aud, exp, iat, roles/permissions snapshot. Signing: HS256 acceptable for monoliths, RS256/ES256 for distributed systems. Refresh token: opaque (not JWT), SHA-256 hashed in DB, single-use with rotation, 7-30 day lifetime. Reuse detection via token families: if rotated token reused, revoke entire family. Clerk takes it further with 60s access tokens eliminating blacklist need. The existing system's HS256 + 15 min + SHA-256 hashed refresh tokens is well-aligned with industry best practices.

key_questions_addressed.question: How should account linking work when a user authenticates via a new provider but already has an identity (email match, explicit linking)?
key_questions_addressed.summary: Two strategies: (1) Automatic: match by verified email, link automatically. Guard: only verified emails auto-link (prevent pre-account takeover). Remove unconfirmed identities on link. (2) Manual: user initiates link while authenticated (linkIdentity). Conflict resolution: if credential already in use, prompt sign-in with existing provider then link. FusionAuth adds Pending Link for deferred decisions. For Telegram (no email): use explicit linking flow where authenticated Telegram user can later link email/OIDC provider.

key_questions_addressed.question: What session management patterns are used in production (sliding expiry, concurrent session limits, device binding)?
key_questions_addressed.summary: Dual timeout model: idle (sliding, 15-30 min general, 2-5 min high-value) + absolute (never extends, 4-8 hours). Session binding: IP + User-Agent + device fingerprint hash. Re-auth on fingerprint mismatch. Concurrent sessions: policy-driven (unlimited, fixed max, terminate oldest, prompt user). Session record: session_id, identity_id, device_fingerprint, ip, user_agent, created_at, last_active_at, idle_expires_at, absolute_expires_at, is_revoked, activated_roles. Store in Redis for fast lookup + PostgreSQL for audit. The existing Session entity needs: last_active_at, idle_expires_at, and device_fingerprint fields.

key_questions_addressed.question: How do IAM systems implement multi-factor authentication and step-up auth in a clean architecture?
key_questions_addressed.summary: Step-up auth triggered by: resource sensitivity, risk signals (new device, unusual location), or explicit policy. Use acr/amr claims in tokens to indicate authentication strength. Keycloak models this as composable authentication flows with conditional execution. MFA factors: TOTP, SMS (discouraged), WebAuthn/passkeys (preferred), email OTP. NIST AAL2 requires phishing-resistant MFA. Implementation: MFAFactor entity per user (type, secret/public_key, enrolled_at, last_used_at). Step-up check: compare token's amr claims against operation's required AAL.

key_questions_addressed.question: What domain events should an Identity bounded context emit (and what are the consumers)?
key_questions_addressed.summary: Core events: IdentityRegistered (-> User module creates Customer/Staff), IdentityAuthenticated (-> audit log, risk engine), ProviderLinked (-> update profile, send notification), ProviderUnlinked (-> update profile), SessionCreated (-> device management), SessionRevoked (-> cleanup), PasswordChanged (-> notify user, invalidate tokens), MFAEnrolled/Removed (-> security log), IdentityDeactivated (-> GDPR cleanup, revoke all sessions), RoleAssignmentChanged (-> invalidate permission cache). The existing system already has: IdentityRegistered, IdentityDeactivated, IdentityReactivated, RoleAssignmentChanged, TelegramIdentityCreated, StaffInvited, StaffInvitationAccepted. Missing: IdentityAuthenticated, ProviderLinked, SessionCreated, SessionRevoked, PasswordChanged.

key_questions_addressed.question: How do production systems handle Telegram Mini App authentication securely (initData validation, freshness, replay prevention)?
key_questions_addressed.summary: Validation: two-step HMAC-SHA256 chain. Step 1: HMAC('WebAppData', bot_token) = intermediate_key. Step 2: HMAC(sorted_pairs_joined_by_newline, intermediate_key) = computed_hash. Compare with provided hash. Freshness: check auth_date against server time, reject if stale (typically >1 hour). Transmission: Authorization header 'tma {initDataRaw}'. Server-side only. Auto-provisioning on first login: create Identity + TelegramCredentials + Customer. Profile sync on subsequent logins. Start_param for referral tracking.

key_questions_addressed.question: What are the OWASP and NIST SP 800-63B requirements for token storage, credential hashing, and session management?
key_questions_addressed.summary: Credential hashing: Argon2id (m=19MiB, t=2, p=1 minimum). Fallback: scrypt > bcrypt (72 byte limit) > PBKDF2. Token storage: HttpOnly + Secure + SameSite cookies (web), OS keychain (mobile). Never localStorage. Session: 64 bits entropy from CSPRNG, regenerate on privilege change, idle timeout 15-30 min, absolute 4-8 hours. NIST AAL-specific: AAL1 max 30 days, AAL2 max 12 hours, AAL3 max 1 hour. PCI DSS: 15 min idle. Generic error messages. Account lockout with exponential backoff. Reauthentication for sensitive operations.

key_questions_addressed.question: How do passkeys/WebAuthn integrate into a multi-provider IAM system alongside traditional auth methods?
key_questions_addressed.summary: WebAuthn is a provider like any other in the identity model. Credential stored as: credential_id (unique), public_key, sign_count (replay detection), transports (usb, nfc, ble, internal), user_verified flag, backed_up flag. Registration: server generates challenge, browser+authenticator create key pair, server stores public key. Authentication: server generates challenge, authenticator signs with private key, server verifies. Can be passwordless (first factor) or MFA (second factor). Syncable passkeys (AAL2) vs device-bound (AAL3). Data model addition: WebAuthnCredential entity with 1:N from Identity.

key_questions_addressed.question: What DDD/Clean Architecture patterns are recommended for modeling IAM (bounded context boundaries, aggregate design, CQRS for auth)?
key_questions_addressed.summary: Identity is a separate bounded context from User. Identity owns: authentication (credentials, sessions, tokens) and authorization (roles, permissions). User owns: profile data (name, email, preferences). Communication via events only. Identity aggregate root contains all credential types as owned entities. One aggregate per transaction. CQRS: Commands for write operations (Login, Register, LinkProvider, RefreshToken, RevokeSession), Queries for reads (GetSession, ListSessions, GetPermissions). Use read models for optimized queries. The existing codebase correctly separates Identity and User modules with event-based communication.
